content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 103v–103r | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_006_page_001.jpg, DEU_006_page_002.jpg; source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_006_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** "Now this is the commandment — the statutes and the ordinances — that the LORD your God commanded me to teach you, so you may follow them in the land you are crossing over to possess. **2** And this is so you and your children and your grandchildren may fear the LORD your God all your days, by keeping all his statutes and his commandments that I am giving you — so that you will live long.

**3** "Hear, O Israel, and be careful to follow them, so that things will go well with you and so you will multiply greatly — just as the LORD, the God of your fathers, promised you — in a land flowing with milk and honey.

**4** "Hear, O Israel: The LORD is our God. The LORD alone.

**5** "Love the LORD your God with all your heart, with all your being, with all your strength.

**6** "These words that I am commanding you today are to be on your heart. **7** Recite them to your children — talk about them when you sit at home and when you walk along the road, when you lie down and when you get up. **8** Tie them as a sign on your hand, and let them be a symbol on your forehead. **9** Write them on the doorposts of your house and on your gates.

**10** "When the LORD your God brings you into the land he swore to your fathers — Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — to give you, with great and flourishing cities you did not build, **11** houses filled with all good things you did not fill, cisterns hewn out that you did not hew, vineyards and olive trees you did not plant — and you eat and are satisfied — **12** then be careful not to forget the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the place of slavery.

**13** "Fear the LORD your God, serve him, and swear by his name alone. **14** Do not follow other gods — gods of the peoples around you. **15** For the LORD your God, who is among you, is a jealous God — and his anger will burn against you, and he will wipe you off the face of the earth.

**16** "Do not put the LORD your God to the test, as you tested him at Massah. **17** Be sure to keep the commands of the LORD your God and the testimonies and statutes he has commanded you. **18** Do what is right and good in the LORD's sight, so that things will go well with you and you will enter and possess the good land the LORD swore to give your fathers — **19** driving out all your enemies before you, as the LORD has said.

**20** "In the future, when your child asks you: 'What do these testimonies, statutes, and ordinances mean that the LORD our God commanded you?' — **21** tell your child: 'We were slaves of Pharaoh in Egypt, and the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand. **22** The LORD worked great and devastating signs and wonders before our eyes — against Egypt and against Pharaoh and against his whole household. **23** And he brought us out from there to bring us in — to give us the land he swore to our fathers. **24** The LORD commanded us to follow all these statutes and to fear the LORD our God for our own good always — so that we might live, as we are today. **25** And this will be our righteousness — to be careful to follow all this commandment before the LORD our God, as he commanded us.'"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Dynamic choices — PNEUMA:**

**V.4 — "Hear, O Israel: The LORD is our God. The LORD alone.":** The Shema. This is the single most important verse in Deuteronomy and arguably in the entire Hebrew Bible. PNEUMA's translation makes a choice: "The LORD alone" rather than "The LORD is one." The Hebrew 'echad can mean either "one" (unity) or "alone" (exclusivity). In context — surrounded by exhortations not to serve other gods — the emphasis is on the LORD's exclusive claim, not merely philosophical monotheism. PNEUMA renders the relational and covenantal force: *this* God, and no other. The Shema is left as a declaration, not an explanation.

**V.5 — "with all your heart, with all your being, with all your strength":** The three-fold address of the commandment to love: lev (heart/mind), nephesh (being/self/life), me'od (strength/might/muchness). "Being" is PNEUMA's consistent rendering for nephesh when the fullness of the person is in view — not just the soul, but the entire living self. "Strength" captures me'od's range: capacity, force, resources. This is a total mobilization of the person toward love.

**V.6 — "These words...are to be on your heart":** Not inscribed, not merely memorized — on your heart (al levavekha). The interior placement signals that these words are to be woven into the fabric of the self. PNEUMA preserves this spatial metaphor because it is foundational to the passage's pedagogy.

**V.7 — "Recite them to your children":** Hebrew veshinantam — literally "sharpen them into" your children. The image is of cutting or engraving. PNEUMA renders "recite" as the natural English equivalent of repeated teaching, but the underlying image is incisive, persistent instruction. The second half of the verse lists four moments: sitting, walking, lying down, rising — the whole cycle of daily life.

**Vv.10–11 — "cities you did not build...cisterns you did not hew":** The gift-economy of Canaan is described as pure grace — inherited wealth, built by others. Israel receives what it did not earn. PNEUMA preserves the triple "you did not" because the point is danger: gratitude is fragile when abundance is assumed to be deserved.

**V.12 — "be careful not to forget":** Hebrew hishamur lekha pen-tishkach — "guard yourself lest you forget." The verb shamar (guard/keep) is applied to the self — a reflexive act of spiritual vigilance. PNEUMA's "be careful not to forget" captures the intentionality required.

**V.16 — "as you tested him at Massah":** A historical reference (Exodus 17:7) placed as a warning. "Do not test as you tested" — the tautology is intentional. PNEUMA preserves Massah as a proper name because it carries its own meaning (testing-place) and is a known reference point.

**V.20 — "In the future, when your child asks you":** The catechetical framework here — child's question, parent's answer — is one of the great pedagogical structures in ancient literature. It reappears at Passover. PNEUMA renders it in natural dialogue form to make the intergenerational transmission vivid.

**V.25 — "this will be our righteousness":** Hebrew tzedaqah — the covenant-conforming rightness of life. Righteousness here is not abstract morality but the relational health of a community aligned with its covenant obligations. PNEUMA keeps "righteousness" rather than the weaker "credit" or "blamelessness" used in some translations — the term is too important to dissolve.

**Vaticanus (LXX) observation:** Vaticanus page 148 covers this chapter. The LXX Shema reads: "Akoue Israel, kyrios ho theos hemon, kyrios heis estin" — "Hear, O Israel, the LORD our God, the LORD is one." The LXX uses "heis" (one) rather than the "alone/exclusive" reading, making an explicit unity claim. This is a significant LXX interpretive decision and shaped how Jewish Greek-speakers and early Christians read the Shema — the NT quotations of the Shema in Mark 12:29 follow this Greek form. At v.5 the LXX adds "ex holes tes dianoias sou" — "with all your mind" — between heart and being/soul. This is the expanded three-part reading that appears in the Synoptic Gospels' four-part version. The Vaticanus page 148 shows CAP.VII notation beginning the next chapter.
